planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring or fall; in zone 9 plant September–March. Epimedium establishes slowly in the first season but spreads steadily thereafter, forming dense, weed-suppressing colonies under trees where other ground covers struggle. Once established, it is remarkably drought tolerant for a shade plant — making it one of the few ground covers that thrives in the dry, shaded conditions under mature trees where soil moisture is depleted by tree roots. Cut back the old foliage to the ground in late winter before new growth emerges to reveal the delicate spring flowers. In zone 9 the foliage is semi-evergreen to nearly evergreen in mild winters.

directions: Plant in shade to dappled shade in well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil — Sulphur Epimedium is exceptional among shade ground covers for its tolerance of dry shade under tree canopies where most ground covers struggle to survive; Sulphureum is one of the most widely planted and reliable Epimedium cultivars, a hybrid ground cover producing heart-shaped, compound leaves on wiry stems with a subtle bronze-red tint on new spring growth and persistent semi-evergreen foliage that provides year-round texture. In early spring, before or with the emerging foliage, delicate sprays of small, spurred, clear sulfur-yellow flowers appear on arching stems — creating a brief but charming floral display that is easily the most delicate and refined spring flowering of any commonly available shade ground cover. Epimedium's genuine value lies in its ability to colonize and thrive under the specific conditions created by large, established shade trees — dry, rooty, shaded soil with heavy competition for moisture and nutrients — conditions where hostas, ferns, and most other shade perennials eventually decline. As a permanent ground cover in these challenging dry-shade situations, Sulphur Epimedium is effectively irreplaceable, providing low-maintenance, visually attractive coverage in the most difficult zone 9 planting conditions.
sources: North Carolina State University Extension — Epimedium for Southern Shade Gardens
University of Georgia Extension — Ground Covers for Dry Shade Under Trees
Missouri Botanical Garden — Epimedium x versicolor Plant Profile

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown plants in spring or fall; in zone 9 plant September–April. Ogon Sweet Flag prefers consistently moist soil and tolerates wet conditions — it grows naturally along stream banks and pond margins. Unlike most ornamental grasses, it thrives in shade and performs poorly in full sun in the South, where heat and sun bleach the golden foliage. Divide every 3–4 years when clumps become crowded. Ogon is excellent in container water gardens, naturalistic pond margins, and as a finely textured edging plant for shaded areas with consistent moisture. The fan-shaped growth pattern is a distinguishing ornamental feature — each clump fans upward from the crown in a flat plane.

directions: Plant in shade to partial shade in consistently moist to wet, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil — Ogon Japanese Sweet Flag thrives in moist, shaded conditions and can grow in shallow water or consistently saturated soil; Ogon is the golden variegated cultivar of Japanese Sweet Flag (Acorus gramineus), producing narrow, strap-like, bright golden-yellow to chartreuse-gold leaves in dense, fan-shaped clumps that grow in a flat plane from the crown — a distinctive, architectural growth form that is unlike any other commonly grown ornamental grass or grass-like plant. The vivid golden-yellow leaf color is exceptionally valuable in the shade garden: the luminous, clean gold provides a strong color accent that brightens dark corners, creates contrast against dark-foliaged companions, and reflects light in a way that makes shaded areas appear more open and bright. Unlike most golden or variegated plants that need sun to maintain their color, Ogon's gold intensity actually improves in shade — the yellow pigmentation is more stable and vivid without the bleaching effect of direct sun. The fine, grass-like texture and modest scale (10–14 inches) make it useful as a textural ground cover, border edging, and pond margin plant in partially shaded locations with adequate moisture.
sources: North Carolina State University Extension — Acorus gramineus 'Ogon' Plant Profile
University of Georgia Extension — Ornamental Grasses for Shade and Moist Conditions
Missouri Botanical Garden — Acorus gramineus Cultivar Information

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring after frost danger has passed; in zone 9 plant February–May. Plant with the neck of the bulb at or just below soil surface — do not bury too deep. Crinum x powellii 'Album' is among the most cold-hardy of all crinum lilies, tolerating temperatures to about 5°F when the bulbs are well-mulched — hardy in zones 7–10 as a permanent landscape bulb. In zone 9 it is essentially evergreen, with the large, strap-like foliage persisting through mild winters. Established clumps multiply freely; dig and divide offsets every 4–5 years when blooming declines. The large bulbs resent disturbance once established — site carefully and allow permanent placement.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained to moist, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil — Crinum x powellii 'Album' adapts to a wide range of soil and light conditions and is one of the most versatile large bulbs for zone 9; White Crinum is one of the most reliably large and spectacular summer-blooming bulbs for Southern gardens, producing huge, fragrant, pure white funnel-shaped flowers (4–5 inches across) on tall, sturdy scapes that emerge above the broad, strap-like foliage in July and August — the peak of summer when most spring bulbs are long finished and fall-blooming plants have not yet begun. Crinum x powellii 'Album' is a hybrid between two South African species that has proven exceptionally cold-hardy for a crinum, extending the growing range well north of most other crinum lilies. The pure white, lily-like flowers (each scape bears 6–12 blooms in succession) are powerfully fragrant in the evening — a feature that makes White Crinum particularly effective near patios, walkways, and outdoor living areas where the fragrance can be appreciated. As a permanent, low-maintenance, long-lived landscape bulb for zone 9 that returns reliably year after year and gradually multiplies into impressive clumps, White Crinum fills the large-scale, summer-blooming bulb niche with a kind of reliable permanence that annual summer bulbs cannot match.
sources: Louisiana State University AgCenter — Crinum Lily for Gulf South Landscapes
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Crinum Bulbs for Florida
North Carolina State University Extension — Hardy Crinum for Southern Gardens

planting.note: Plant transplants after all frost danger has passed; in zone 9 plant March–May. Unlike Impatiens walleriana (common impatiens), New Guinea impatiens tolerate and actually perform well in partial sun to full sun conditions — they are not exclusively shade plants. The Infinity series was bred specifically for heat tolerance and disease resistance, including resistance to downy mildew (Plasmopara destructor) that devastated common impatiens plantings across North America in 2011–2014. In zone 9 the Infinity series blooms continuously from spring planting through frost with minimal care, making them one of the most reliable continuous-blooming annuals for the Gulf South.

directions: Plant in partial shade to partial sun (2–4 hours of direct sun) in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral, moist soil — Infinity New Guinea Impatiens performs best with morning sun and afternoon shade in zone 9's climate; Infinity is a New Guinea impatiens series from PanAmerican Seed that represents the modern generation of improved, heat-tolerant, disease-resistant New Guinea impatiens — a step change in reliability and performance over older New Guinea varieties that often struggled in the combination of heat, humidity, and downy mildew pressure that characterizes Gulf South summer conditions. New Guinea impatiens (Impatiens hawkeri), unlike the common bedding impatiens (I. walleriana), has significantly better heat tolerance and sun tolerance — growing and flowering satisfactorily in conditions that would cause common impatiens to collapse. The large, flat, single or semi-double flowers (3–4 inches across in some colors) in a wide range of solids and bicolors bloom continuously and prolifically without deadheading. For zone 9 gardeners frustrated by the decline of common impatiens in summer heat and the susceptibility to downy mildew, Infinity New Guinea impatiens provides a genuine, heat-adapted alternative for partial-shade color through the longest and hottest part of the growing season.
sources: PanAmerican Seed — Infinity New Guinea Impatiens Series
University of Florida IFAS Extension — New Guinea Impatiens for Florida
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Heat-Tolerant Shade Annuals for Gulf South

planting.note: Move outdoors to a shaded location after temperatures remain above 55°F; in zone 9 place outdoors April–October and overwinter indoors. Medallion Calathea requires consistent temperatures above 60°F and is damaged by temperatures below 50°F — manage as an outdoor summer container plant in zone 9 and bring indoors before fall temperatures drop. Maintain consistently moist soil and high humidity; brown leaf edges indicate low humidity or fluoride/chlorine sensitivity in tap water (use filtered or rainwater when possible). Zone 9's warm, humid summers create ideal outdoor conditions on shaded patios from May through September. The leaves track light through the day and fold partially upward at night.

directions: Plant in deep shade to bright indirect light — no direct sun — in well-drained, humus-rich, consistently moist, slightly acidic soil; Medallion Calathea produces some of the most spectacular patterned foliage of any commonly available houseplant or shade tropical, with large, rounded leaves displaying a complex, symmetrical pattern of dark green and lighter green ovals, chevrons, and feathered markings on the upper surface and deep burgundy-purple undersides. The Medallion pattern — a central lighter zone feathered with dark green radiating from the leaf's midrib, surrounded by progressively darker zones toward the margin — is strikingly ornate and immediately recognizable as one of the most decorative naturally occurring leaf patterns. In zone 9 Medallion Calathea provides an extraordinary level of ornamental interest as an outdoor container plant for covered patios and deep porches during the warm season, where the plant thrives in the humid warmth while providing foliage drama that no temperate shade plant can match. The leaves perform a nyctinastic movement — folding slightly upward and revealing the burgundy underside at night, then relaxing back to horizontal during the day — an unusual biological behavior that adds an additional dimension of interest beyond the static foliage pattern.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Calathea and Goeppertia Species for Florida
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Tropical Foliage Plants for Zone 9 Patios
American Botanical Council — Calathea ornamental foliage documentation
